coredata.dc:title: Optically induced insulator-to-semiconductor transition in fluorescent carbon quantum dots measured by terahertz time-domain spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Dan
Tian, Jiekang
Xu, Wen
Wen, Hua
Wang, Chao
Tang, Ju
Zhang, Jin
Guo, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescent carbon quantum dots (FCQDs) are promising for wide applications especially in the field of optoelectronic materials and devices. For these applications, it is essential to examine the optoelectronic properties of FCQDs not only in a solution but also in the form of dry particles. Here we study the optoelectronic properties of dry FCQDs by using terahertz (THz) time-domain spectroscopy (TDS). The THz response of dry FCQDs, which can emit blue (B-CQDs) and red (R-CQDs) lights in solutions under optical excitation, has been measured in temperature range from 80 to 280 K. We find that R-CQDs behaves like an optical insulator in 0.2–1.2 THz range, while B-CQDs experiences insulator-to-semiconductor transition with increasing THz frequency and temperature. The optical conductance and the key sample parameters of B-CQDs are determined. This is the first research work in applying the THz TDS to study the dry CQDs. The findings from this study can be helpful in gaining an in-depth understanding of the physical properties of dry FCQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Terahertz time-domain spectroscopy
Insulator-to-semiconductor transition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical conductance and key sample parameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of the THz TDS system is shown on the left and the sketch of tested sample, CQDs with attached chemical elements is shown on the right. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

HRTEM images of B-CQDs in (a) and R-CQDs in (b). (c) FTIR spectra of B- (blue curve) and R-CQDs (red curve), where the characteristic bands induced by different vibration modes are indicated. (d) Full-scan XPS spectrum of B-CQDs and the high resolution XPS spectra for C1s (e), N1s (f) and O1s (g). (h) Full-scan XPS spectrum of R-CQDs and the high resolution XPS spectra for C1s (i), N1s (j) and O1s (k). The deconvolutions of the high resolution XPS spectra are also presented accordingly. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

PL (CQDs in solutions, upper panel) and UV–Vis absorption [CQDs in solutions (solid curves) and as dry powder (dashed curves), lower panel) spectra are shown in (a) for B-CQDs and in (b) for R-CQDs. (c) The diagram of the energy levels and the electronic transition channels for PL emission from B- (left panel) and R-CQDs (right panel). The black lines refer to energy levels for carrier transitions, the dashed black line in right panel refers to the energy level with carrier traps, and the grey lines refer to the radiative energy levels. Red lines with arrows refer to the major channels of electronic transition (strong peaks), while the blue lines with arrows refer to less possible electronic transition channels (weak peaks). Dashed lines with arrows represent the non-radiative electronic transitions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Spectra of the transmittance induced by R-CQDs (dotted curves) and B-CQDs (solid curves) at different temperatures as indicated. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Real [
σ 1
( ω )
,
blue color] and imaginary [
σ 2
( ω )
, red color] parts of optical conductance for B-CQDs as a function of radiation frequency
f = ω / 2 π
at different temperatures as indicated. Here, the experimental and theoretical results are represented respectively with diamonds and solid curves. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The ratio of the carrier density to effective electron mass
R = g
n e
/
m ∗
(upper panel), the electronic relaxation time
τ
(lower panel) and the electronic localization factor c (inset) as a function of temperature for B-CQDs. The symbols are obtained via fitting the experimental results with the DSF, the curves in the upper and lower panels are given respectively by Eq. (4) and Eq. (5), and the curve in the inset is drown to guide the eye. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Optically induced insulator-to-semiconductor transition in fluorescent carbon quantum dots measured by terahertz time-domain spectroscopy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Contribution made equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent carbon quantum dots (FCQDs) are promising for wide applications especially in the field of optoelectronic materials and devices. For these applications, it is essential to examine the optoelectronic properties of FCQDs not only in a solution but also in the form of dry particles. Here we study the optoelectronic properties of dry FCQDs by using terahertz (THz) time-domain spectroscopy (TDS). The THz response of dry FCQDs, which can emit blue (B-CQDs) and red (R-CQDs) lights in solutions under optical excitation, has been measured in temperature range from 80 to 280 K. We find that R-CQDs behaves like an optical insulator in 0.2–1.2 THz range, while B-CQDs experiences insulator-to-semiconductor transition with increasing THz frequency and temperature. The optical conductance and the key sample parameters of B-CQDs are determined. This is the first research work in applying the THz TDS to study the dry CQDs. The findings from this study can be helpful in gaining an in-depth understanding of the physical properties of dry FCQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional surface-passivated materials with typical sizes of several nanometers. They can exhibit excellent optical and optoelectronic properties, such as tunable photoluminescence (PL) from deep ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) [1,2], long-term stability [3], high fluorescent quantum yield [4], ultrafast electron injection and extraction [5], to mention but a few. These outstanding features made the CQDs available for wide applications, especially in the field of optoelectronic materials and devices. In particular, light-emitting diode (LED) is one of the most important device applications of the fluorescent CQDs (FCQDs), which can be utilized for the realization of full-color lighting and displays [6–9].

Up to now, most published research work on optical and optoelectronic properties of FCQDs has been carried out for the situation where the FCQDs are in solutions [10–13] such as water, NaOH, etc. For practical applications of FCQDs as the materials for LED and display, the FCQDs should be used in the form of dry particles or powder. Due to the quantum confinement effect, the FCQDs behave like a semiconductor with direct band-gaps induced by conjugated carbon π-domains which can be tuned by varying the size and geometry of the carbon dots [11]. Furthermore, experimental evidence shows that the fluorescent characteristics of the CQDs heavily depends on the presence of the chemical functional groups attached to the surface/edges of the carbon cores in CQDs [12,13]. More importantly, when FCQDs are in the form of dry particles in making, e.g., LEDs, their electronic and optical properties may differ greatly from the situation when putting them in a solution [10]. Therefore, it is of great significance and importance to examine the optoelectronic properties of FCQDs in the form of dry particles.

At present, terahertz (THz) time-domain spectroscopy (TDS) [14] has become a powerful and popularly used optical technique in studying optoelectronic properties of electronic materials such as semiconductors, metals, oxides, superconductors, etc. This non-contacting technique can be applied to optically measure the key sample parameters of an electronic material [15,16] and, thus, it has been widely used to investigate the optoelectronic properties of nanomaterials such as quantum dots [17], nanoparticles [18], nanowires [19], carbon-nanotube [20], micro/nanostructured Au sphere arrays [21], graphene [22], monolayer (ML) MoS2 [23], ML WS2 [24], ML h-BN [25], and so on. In this work, we would like to apply this advanced technique for the measurement of the optoelectronic properties of dry FCQDs for the first time.

In this study, we synthesize the FCQDs which can emit bright blue and red lights in solutions under optical excitation. We then make them into the form of dry particles for THz measurement. In principle, the CQDs are nanoparticles with conducting carriers. From a physics standpoint, the THz optoelectronic response of the CQDs should be different from that of the noble metal (e.g., gold and silver) based nanoparticles whose optical properties are mainly affected by plasmon and surface plasmon effects [26]. Moreover, when the size of the carbon dots approaches to the nanometer distance scale, which is smaller than the mean-free path of the electron motion in the carbon core [27], the direct current (dc) electrical conduction can be reduced pronouncedly. However, we found that in the presence of THz irradiation, the metal-insulator transition can be observed in micro/nanostructured Au sphere arrays [21] and such an effect heavily depends on the diameter and thickness of the Au sphere. This implies that THz optoelectronic response from nanostructured electronic systems can have some unique features. In the present study, we intend to examine how the dry FCQDs can respond to THz radiation field and to determine optically the sample parameters of dry CQDs.

In this study, we prepared two kinds of the FCQDs which can generate blue (named as B-CQDs) and red (named as R-CQDs) light emission in respectively ethyl acetate and ethanol solutions under UV irradiation. These CQDs were synthesized by a standard solvothermal method [28]. A mixed solution of p-Phenylenediamine (100 mg), urea (100 mg), and dimethyl sulfoxide (10 mL) was prepared as basic feedstock. The B-CQDs were synthesized directly from the basic feedstock, while the R-CQDs were synthesized from a mixed solution with 200 μL of H2O2 added to the basic feedstock. The as-prepared solutions were transferred into to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (25 mL). After heating at 200 °C for 3 h and then naturally cooling to room temperature, the crude B- and R-CQDs were obtained respectively. They were purified by silica column chromatography using a mixture of ethyl acetate and ethanol (20:1) as an eluent for two rounds. Then they were dispersed into the ethyl acetate (B-CQDs) and ethanol (R-CQDs) solutions respectively to achieve a good solubility. Thus, two kinds of samples, B- and R-CQDs, can be synthesized. The size of the CQDs obtained from this technique can be controlled via, e.g., varying the growth temperature and the reaction time.

To get the dry samples, the solutions with FCQDs were dripped onto the 10 mm ⅹ 10 mm ⅹ 0.5 mm quartz slides with a dropper and then dried spontaneously. After 6 drops for each sample, the solution with the FCQDs in a total volume around 0.3 mL was dried as particles or powders. A quartz cover slide was placed on top of the powders and fixed by paste tap at the edges, to prevent the drop and the movement of the dots during the THz TDS measurement.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100, Japan), operated at 200 kV, was used to take the images of the CQDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, FRONTIER, PerkinElmer) spectroscopy and the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis, HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA) spectroscopy were applied for measuring the infrared transmission and UV–Vis absorption of the CQDs. The x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+) was used to obtain the x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CQDs. The PL spectra were measured for CQDs in a standard experimental setup (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA).

In the present study, the experimental setup for the THz TDS is shown in Fig. 1 for transmission measurement [22,24]. Briefly (see Fig. 1), i) 80 fs fiber laser beam, with 100 MHz pulse repetition rate and central wavelength 1.56 μm, is divided into two beams as the pump and probe light sources respectively; ii) the pump beam is focused on an InGaAs photoconductive antenna (PCA) to generate the pulsed THz wave with a spectral range from 0.1 THz to 2.0 THz; iii) the generated THz beam is focused perpendicularly on the surface of the sample; iv) after the time delay by a delay stage, the probe laser beam is focused on another InGaAs PCA for the detection of the THz beam transmitted through the sample via the photoconductive sampling; v) the sample is placed on the sample holder in a cryostat with quartz windows. Because the quartz windows are used in the cryostat, which absorb the high frequency THz light, the effective spectrum content of the THz beam focused on the sample is limited within 0.2 THz to about 1.2 THz. The vacuum degree inside the cryostat is maintained at about 10−7 mbar during the measurements; and vi) the measurement temperature is kept between 80 K and 280 K by using liquid nitrogen.

In Fig. 2 (a) and (b), we show the HRTEM images of the B- and R-CQDs respectively. From these images, the average size of the CQDs can be measured statistically over roughly a hundred particles by using the Image J software. We find that the average diameters are 3.71 ± 0.22 nm for B-CQDs and 4.90 ± 0.22 nm for R-CQDs, respectively. The HRTEM images map the obvious lattice structures in carbon cores of the CQDs and two kinds of lattices with average lattice spacing of 0.19 nm (0.21 nm) and 0.32 nm (0.32 nm) for B-CQDs (R-CQDs) were identified. The lattice constants of 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm correspond respectively to the (100)- and (002)-facet spacing of the carbon dot [29,30]. These results indicate that the CQDs used in this study are with good C-based crystallographic structures.

The FTIR spectra for the B- and R-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2(c), where the absorption bands around 950–1120 cm−1, 1255 cm−1 and 1346-1459 cm−1 (with a double wavenumber at 2815-2991 cm−1) are attributed respectively to the stretching vibrations of C–O, bending vibrations of –OH and stretching vibrations of C–N bonds [31,32]. We notice that the stretching vibration of the C–N bond is much stronger for R-CQDs than that for B-CQDs. Moreover, compared to the B-CQDs, the R-CQDs show two extra absorption peaks located at 1623 cm−1 and 3134-3429 cm−1, which relate respectively to stretching vibrations of CO and N–H bonds. These results imply that the chemical bonds attached to the edges of the R- and B-CQDs are different.

The full XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 2(d) and (h) for respectively the B- and R-CQDs. From the peaks at 284.8, 399.3 and 532.1 eV, we learn that the B-CQDs mainly consist of C (70.06%), N (0.85%) and O (29.09%), while the R-CQDs mainly consist of C (74.16%), N (4.04%) and O (21.8%). The N content in R-CQDs is much higher than that in B-CQDs, in line with the results from FTIR as shown in Fig. 2(c). For B-CQDs, we note that: i) the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2(e)) can be deconvoluted into the contributions from C–C/CC (284.7 eV, area percentage 94.64%), C–O/C–N (286.3 eV, 4.11%) and CO (287.8 eV, 1.25%); ii) the O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2(f) can be fitted with two contributions from C–O and CO bonds; and iii) the N1s spectrum in Fig. 2(g) can be fitted with two contributions peaked at 398.3 eV and 399.3 eV, which are assigned to pyridinic N and amino N respectively. For R-CQDs, i) the high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(i) can be deconvoluted into four contributions from C–C/CC (284.7 eV, area percentage 89.41%), C–O/C–N (286.3 eV, 6.66%), carbonyl carbons CO (287.8 eV, 2.62%) and carboxyl carbons O–CO (corresponding to –COOH, 288.9 eV, 1.30%); ii) the O1s spectrum in Fig. 2(j) can be fitted with two contributions from C–O and CO; and iii) the N1s spectrum in Fig. 2(k) can be deconvoluted into three contributions peaked respectively at 398.3 eV for pyridinic N, 399.3 eV for amino N and 401.2 eV for graphitic N [13,31]. These results indicate that the R-CQDs have extra N-based bonds and have a lager content of CO, O–CO (corresponding to –COOH groups) bonds, in comparison to the B-CQDs.

The PL and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the B- and R-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . From the PL spectra shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b), we find that the PL peaks are located at 457 nm for B-CQDs and at 614 nm for R-CQDs respectively. They do not shift with varying the excitation wavelength. The PL excitation (PLE) spectra show two peaks for B-CQD with a weaker peak at 279 nm and a stronger one at 369 nm and for R-CQDs where the peak located at 286 nm is remarkably stronger than that at 528 nm, implying that there are two electronic transition channels for PL emission. In the lower panels in Fig. 3(a) and (b), the UV–Vis absorption spectra of B- and R-CQDs are shown for CQDs in solutions (solid curves) and as dry powders (dashed curves). Two absorption peaks can be measured for both B- and R-CQDs in solutions. The UV–Vis absorption, at about 279 nm for B-CQDs and 288 nm for R-CQDs, is mainly induced via band-gap transition of conjugated π-domains [33]. The longer-wavelength absorption, around 369 nm for B-CQDs and 528 nm for R-CQDs, is caused mainly by the chemical bonds attached to the CQDs [10,12,13]. These absorption peaks are in line with the PLE peaks. It should be pointed out that in order to see the absorption peaks more markedly, the absorption spectra (solid curves) are obtained by using the solutions with higher and lower CQDs concentrations in, respectively, long and short wavelength regimes. We note that the peaks of UV–Vis absorption spectra of B- and R-CQDs for CQD powders are in consist with those in solutions, although the absorption peak for R-CQD powder at about 528 nm is not so obviously. This suggests that the intrinsic electronic structure does not change significantly when the CQDs are in the form of dry powders. The relatively weaker absorption spectra of CQD powders (dashed curves) than those for CQDs in solutions (solid curves) are mainly caused by the effect of diffuse reflection of the powder particles. Moreover, we find that the PL emission from both B- and R-CQD powders is very weak and almost unmeasurable under the current experimental conditions. From the intensity and the wavelength of the PL spectra, we see that the PL emission from B-CQDs is mainly dominant by the chemical bonds attached to the CQDs [10,12,13], while the PL emission from R-CQDs comes mainly from the band-gap transition of conjugated π-domains [33]. From these results, we can draw the diagram of the electronic energy levels and corresponding transition channels for PL emission from B- and R-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 3(c). We note the following points. i) Because the B-CQDs are smaller than the R-CQDs (see Fig. 2(a)), the energy spacing between the HOMO and the LUMO states for B-CQDs (about 279 nm) is larger than that for R-CQDs (about 288 nm). ii) For B- and R-CQDs, there are two channels for optical pumping and electronic relaxation. An intermediate state, induced by chemical bonds attached to the CQDs, serves for non-radiative electronic transition to lower energy levels. iii) For B-CQDs, the intermediate state do not trap electrons significantly so that the strong PL emission can be observed for electronic transition from intermediate state to lower and radiative level (grey line). In contrast, for R-CQDs the intermediate state, mainly induced by CO, –COOH and N-related bonds confirmed by XPS and FTIR results, can trap electrons significantly. Thus, the weak PL emission can be observed for electronic transition from intermediate state to lower and radiative level (grey line). Hence, the strong PL emission from R-CQDs is related to electronic transition from LUMO to radiative level (grey line). And iv) the mechanisms of the PL emission from B- and R-CQDs are akin to those from impurity states in a direct band-gap semiconductor with non-radiative and radiative levels [34,35].

The above results indicate that the main differences of the B- and R-CQDs used in this study are their sizes, the chemical function groups attached to the cores of the CQDs, and the features of the intermediate levels for non-radiative electronic transitions. In particular, we would like to point out here again that in comparison to the B-CQDs, the R-CQDs have relatively lager contents of CO, –COOH and –NH2 bonds along with the extra bonding states of graphitic N.

Applying the THz TDS technique, we can measure the strength of the THz electric field transmitted through a sample in time-domain
E
( t )
. We first measure the transmission signals for two quartz slides as the substrate,
E s
( t )
. Then we measure the transmission signals for CQDs sandwiched between two quartz slides,
E
c s
( t )
. Both
E s
( t )
and
E
c s
( t )
are measured at the same temperature. The corresponding strengths of the THz electric field in frequency-domain,
E s
( ω )
and
E
c s
( ω )
, can be obtained respectively via Fourier transformation of
E s
( t )
and
E
c s
( t )
. The amplitude and the phase angle of
E
c s
( ω )
for B-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 at different temperatures from 80 K to 280 K. As we can see, varying temperature affects mainly the amplitude of
E
c s
( ω )
, while the phase angle of
E
c s
( ω )
depends very little on temperature.

From the results obtained for
E s
( ω )
and
E
c s
( ω )
, we can get the net contribution to THz transmittance from CQDs alone via: T ( ω ) ≈
E
c s
( ω )
2
/
E s
( ω )
2
with
|
E
( ω )
|
being the amplitude of the corresponding THz field in frequency-domain. In Fig. 5 , we show the spectra of T(ω) for R- and B-CQDs at different temperatures from 80 to 280 K. It should be noted that T(ω) for R-CQDs is slightly larger than 1. This is mainly due to the fact that in the derivation of T ( ω ) ≈
E
c s
( ω )
2
/
E s
( ω )
2
, the effect of light reflection from the substrate and from the CQDs has been neglected [36]. From Fig. 4, we see that the transmittance for R-CQDs is about 1 in the frequency and temperature ranges from 0.2 to 1.2 THz and from 80 to 280 K. For an electronic system placed on a substrate, the relationship between the transmittance and the optical conductance
σ
( ω )
is given by:
(1)
T
ω
=
ε 2
ε 1
4
ε 1
ε 0
2
|
ε 1
ε 2
+
ε 1
ε 0
+
ε 1
σ ( ω ) /
c 0
|
2
where
ε 1
≈ 1
for air,
ε 2
is the effective high-frequency dielectric constant of the substrate, and
ε 0
and
c 0
are the dielectric constant and the speed of light in vacuum respectively. From this relation, we have
σ
( ω )
→0 when
T
( ω )
→1. Thus, the electronic system is in an insulating state and the sample is transparent fully for the incident light beam. On the other hand, the transmittance for the B-CQDs approaches to 1 at low-frequencies (e.g., around 0.2 THz) and low-temperatures (e.g., at 80 K, red solid curve) and it decreases with increasing radiation frequency and temperature. This implies that with increasing frequency and temperature, the carriers in the B-CQDs become conducting optically so that
T
( ω )
is obviously less than 1. Consequently, the B-CQDs experience optically induced transition from an insulator to a conductor with increasing radiation frequency and temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical conductance and key sample parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
From
E s
( ω )
and
E
c s
( ω )
obtained respectively for the substrate and for the sample, we can determine the complex optical conductance σ
ω
=
σ 1
ω
+ i
σ 2
ω
for CQDs through Tinkham formula [37].
(2)
E
c s
( ω )
E s
( ω )
=
1 + n
1 + n +
Z 0
σ
( ω )
where n = 1.96 is the refractive index of the quartz substrate [38] and Z 0 = 377 Ω is the impedance of free space. Because the real
σ 1
( ω )
and imaginary
σ 2
( ω )
parts of the optical conductance for R-CQDs are roughly zero in f = 0.2–1.2 THz range, below we focus our attention mainly no the situation of B-CQDs. The real (blue diamonds) and imaginary (red diamonds) parts of the optical conductance obtained experimentally for B-CQDs are shown in Fig. 6 as a function of radiation frequency (f =
ω /
2
π
) at different temperatures from 80 K to 280 K. We can see that for B-CQDs,
σ 1
( ω )
increases and
σ 2
( ω )
decreases with increasing f and
σ 2
( ω )
is negative at the measured frequency and temperature ranges. Such behaves disobey obviously the Drude formula for optical conductance in an electron gas system, which reads:
σ
( ω )
=
σ 0
/
( 1 − i ω τ )
with
σ 0
= g
n e
e 2
τ /
m ∗
being the dc conductance,
n e
the carrier density,
τ
the electronic relaxation time,
m ∗
the effective electron mass, and
g
the geometry factor to determine the dc conductivity Σ0 =
σ 0
/ g
. The conventional Drude formula suggests that
σ 1
( ω )
should decrease with increasing
ω
or f and
σ 2
( ω )
should always be positive. Furthermore, it should be noted from Fig. 6 that both
σ 1
( ω )
→0 and
σ 2
( ω )
→0 can be measured at low-frequency regime, which confirms that the electrons in B-CQDs experience the insulator to conductor transition with increasing radiation frequency. This effect is more notable at relatively low-temperatures.

In this study, we employ the modified Drude-Smith formula (DSF) [39] for the understanding of the experimental results shown in Fig. 6, which is given as
(3)
σ
( ω )
=
σ 0
1 − i ω τ
[
1 +
c
( 1 − i ω τ )
]
,
where a coefficient
c ∈
[ − 1,0 ]
denotes the electronic localization factor induced by subsequent collisions between electrons and scattering centers due to backscattering mechanism with a Poisson statistics [39]. The DSF has been widely applied to examine THz optoelectronic properties of nanostructure systems [23–25]. We note that Eq. (3) includes only the first collision term in the general DSF, with which we are already able to achieve a good fitting between the experimental and theoretical results (see Fig. 6). When taking the c-factor in Eq. (3) to be zero, the DSF becomes the conventional Drude formula. The discrepancy between experimental data and the Drude formula can be settled with the Drude-Smith formula. The real and imaginary parts of the optical conductance obtained from the DSF are also shown in Fig. 6.

Through fitting the experimental results for the real and imaginary parts of the optical conductance of the CQDs with the DSF, we can determine the key sample and material parameters such as the ratio of the carrier density to the effective electron mass
R = g
n e
/
m ∗
, the electronic relaxation time τ, and the electronic localization factor c. We note that at present the effective mass m∗ for an electron in a CQD is unknown. Moreover, here we cannot figure out if the B-CQD sample is n- or p-type and how the geometry factor
g
is for the B-CQDs. Therefore, we take
R = g
n e
/
m ∗
to see the change of the carrier density with varying the temperature on the basis that the effective electron mass and the geometry factor do not alter significantly with varying the temperature.

In Fig. 7 , we plot
R = g
n e
/
m ∗
, τ and c as a function of temperature for B-CQDs. We find that R or n
e increases with temperature and the temperature dependence of
R = g
n e
/
m ∗
can be fitted nicely by the Arrhenius relation:
(4)
A = a ⋅ exp
( − Δ E /
k B
T )
where
a =
7.85ⅹ1049 cm−2kg−1 and the activation energy
Δ E
= 10.77 meV (see blue curve in Fig. 7). It should be noted that Eq. (4) describes the typical dependence of the carrier density upon the temperature for a semiconductor [40]. Consequently, in the presence of THz radiation and at relatively high temperatures, the carriers in B-CQDs have a semiconductor-like electronic behavior with an activation energy about 10.77 meV
Δ E
= 10.77 meV closes to a value of 15 meV reported for semiconductor-based quantum dots [41]. A relatively low
Δ E
found in B-CQDs implies that the B-CQDs are of remarkable ability for carrier injection and transfer. This finding is in line with the experimental results obtained for graphene-based quantum dots (GQDs) [5]. From Fig. 7, we see that the electronic relaxation time τ decreases with increasing T and the temperature dependence of τ can be fitted very well to a formula given by Ref. [42,43] (see red curve in Fig. 7):
(5)
1 τ
=
1
τ I
+
α
A C
T +
Γ LO
( exp
(
E LO
/
k B
T )
− 1 )
− 1
for carrier-impurity (the first term), carrier-acoustic phonon (the second term) and carrier-longitudinal optical (LO) phonon (the third term) scattering in GQDs. Here
τ I
,
α
A C
and
Γ LO
are temperature-independent coefficients for different scattering centers and
E LO
is the LO phonon energy. For GQDs
E LO
=
198.8 meV determined from G-band Raman shift [44], and we take this value for fitting of the B-CQDs. By taking
τ I
= 26.95 fs,
α
A C
= 3.54ⅹ10−5 fs−1 and
Γ LO
= 49.44 fs−1, the best fitting can be achieved as shown in the lower panel in Fig. 7. The electronic relaxation time decreases with increasing temperature, induced mainly via carrier-phonon scattering, is another typical feature for a semiconductor. This reconfirms that in the presence of THz radiation and at relatively high temperatures, the carriers in B-CQDs have a semiconductor-like electronic behavior. Furthermore, from the inset in Fig. 7 we find that the values of the electronic localization factor c are close to −1 in temperature range from 80 K to 280 K. Because the c-factor describes the fraction of original velocity for an electron after a collision event, which reflects the effect of electronic localization induced by electronic backscattering in the presence of the radiation field,
c ∼ − 1
implies that the carriers in B-CQDs are highly localized due to optically induced electronic backscattering [39].

From the DSF given by Eq. (3), we learn that the optical conductance at low-frequency limit is:
σ
(
ω → 0
)
=
σ 0
( 1 + c )
. For B-CQDs, because
c ∼ − 1
,
σ
(
ω → 0
)
∼ 0
. Thus, the insulating state in B-CQDs at low-frequencies can be understood by the effect of optically induced electronic backscattering or localization. This result also indicates that the B-CQDs are not conducting under a dc electric field. The physical reason behind this feature is that the size of the B-CQDs is about 3.71 ± 0.22 nm, which is smaller than the mean-free path of the electron motion in carbon cores (normally in sub-μm range). Thus, the dc electric conduction can be reduced pronouncedly due to quantum confinement [27].

The results from this study indicate that the optical insulating state can be more pronounced observed at relatively low-temperatures. This is because a low carrier density can be achieved at a lower temperature (see Fig. 7) and, thus, a lower carrier density corresponds to a smaller optical conductance. In the presence of THz radiation and at relatively high-temperatures, the CQDs behave like a semiconductor. In such a case, the conducting carriers can be thermally generated and the carriers can be scattered via carrier-impurity and carrier-phonon interactions. With increasing temperature, the carrier-phonon scattering becomes stronger (i.e., with a shorter
τ
) so that optical absorption increases in the CQDs. As a result, the corresponding optical conductance increases with temperature, because
σ
( ω )
∼
[ 1 +
(
ω τ
)
2
]
− 1
.

The results obtained from the characterization of the R- and B-CQDs show that in contrast to the B-CQDs, the chemical bonds or functional groups attached to the surface and edges of the carbon cores in the R-CQDs, such as the CO, –COOH and –NH2 bonds, along with extra graphitic N bonding states, can lead to the trapping of the carriers in the CQDs and to the reduction of the dc conductance. Therefore, the dc conductance and the optical conductance in R-CQDs approach to vanish. This is the main reason why the R-CQDs are an optical insulator in THz range. In contrast, because of relatively less and weaker chemical bonds presented in the B-CQDs (see Fig. 2), the effect of the trapping of the conducting carriers in B-CQDs is relatively weaker. The insulator-to-semiconductor transition can be observed optically in B-CQDs in THz range. These results can also be used to understand the different PL behaviors between R- and B-CQDs.

In this study, we demonstrate that the optoelectronic properties of the FCQDs depend strongly on the frequency of the THz wave and the temperature. The effect of the insulator-to-semiconductor transition observed for dry CQDs can be applied to modulate the optical transmission in THz bandwidth. Thus, the CQDs can be utilized to realize the advanced THz optoelectronic materials and devices. Moreover, this study is the first to implement the THz spectroscopy for FCQDs. Since the THz TDS is powerful and practical tool to study the electronic dynamical properties of the quantum confinement systems such as the FCQDs, this technique can be applied for the investigation of other quantum dot (QD) systems such as PbS- and CeO2-based QDs and QD nanocomposites [45–49].

In this work, we have synthesized the fluorescent carbon quantum dots (FCQDs) which can emit strong blue (B-CQDs) and red (R-CQDs) lights in solutions under optical excitation. These CQDs have been characterized and been made in the form of dry particles. We have measured their optoelectronic properties by using THz time-domain spectroscopy (TDS). The main findings and conclusions obtained from this study can be summarized as follows.
(1)
The size, the chemical bonds attached to the cores of the CQDs, and the features of the intermediate levels for non-radiative electronic transitions in B- and R-CQDs used in this study are significantly different. These differences are the main reasons why the B- and R-CQDs can show different features of the optoelectronic response.
(2)
In 0.2 to 1.2 THz and 80–280 K ranges, the optical conductance in R-CQDs approaches to vanish so that the R-CQDs are in an optical insulator state in THz regime. The main physical reason behind this effect is that the strong chemical bonds attached to the edges of the carbon cores in the R-CQDs, which can trap the conducting carriers in the R-CQDs.
(3)
In 0.2 to 1.2 THz and 80–280 K ranges, the transmittance for B-CQDs approaches 1 at relatively low-frequencies and low-temperatures. The corresponding optical conductance increases with increasing temperature and radiation frequency. Thus, the B-CQDs experience optically an insulator-to-semiconductor transition.
(4)
We have demonstrated that by using THz TDS measurement, we can obtain the complex optical conductance for FCQDs. Via fitting the real and imaginary parts of the optical conductance obtained experimentally with the Drude-Smith formula, we are able to determine optically the key sample and material parameters for FCQDs. We have examined the temperature dependence of these parameters for B-CQDs, confirming that the FCQDs can behave like a semiconductor not only when they are in a solution, but also when they are in a form of dry particles.

The results obtained from this work indicate that the field of the investigation of the FCQDs is very rich in terms of physics. In particular, the optical insulator-to-semiconductor transition is a new physics phenomenon observed for CQDs. We hope these important and significant experimental findings from this work can help us to gain an in-depth understanding of the basic physical properties of the FCQDs, especially of the CQD dry particles for potential applications as optical and optoelectronic materials and devices.

Dan Song: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, THz TDS measurement and data analysis, write manuscript. Jiekang Tian: Formal analysis, sample preparation, characterization and analysis (PL, UV–Vis, XPS, FTIR). Wen Xu: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Proposed the research work, analyses the results, write and revise the manuscript. Hua Wen: THz TDS experiment and data treatment. Chao Wang: Formal analysis, THz TDS experiment and sample analysis. Ju Tang: sample characterization (TEM). Jin Zhang: sample characterization (TEM, XPS). Mingming Guo: sample preparation and characterization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science foundation of China (U2067207, U1930116, 81127901) and the Center of Science and Technology of Hefei Academy of Science (2016FXZY002).

Pathan, PbS sensitized TiO2 based quantum dot solar cells with efficiency greater than 5 % under artificial light: effect of compact layer and surface passivation, Eng. Sci. 7 (2019) 38-42. https://doi.org/10.30919/es8d676.
